analogy: Think of "Federalism" through the chapter's own opening images: 150 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Seven FEDERALISM INTRODUCTION Look at the political maps (on next two pages) of India 1947 and 2017. They have changed dramatically over the years. Boundaries of States have changed, names of States have changed, and the number of States... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: FEDERALISM, INTRODUCTION, INDIA IN 1947, WHAT IS FEDERALISM?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Federalism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 150 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Seven FEDERALISM INTRODUCTION Look at the political maps (on next two pages) of India 1947 and 2017.

The text explains that they have changed dramatically over the years.

The text explains that boundaries of States have changed, names of States have changed, and the number of States has changed.

The text explains that when India became independent, we had a number of provinces that the British government had organised only for administrative convenience.

The text explains that then a number of princely states merged with the newly independent Indian union.

The text explains that since then boundaries of States have been reorganised many times.

The text explains that during this entire period, not only did boundaries of States change, but in some cases, even their names changed according to the wishes of the people of those States.

The text explains that thus, Mysore changed to Karnataka and Madras became Tamil Nadu.

The text explains that the maps show these large scale changes that have taken place in the span of over seventy years.

The text explains that in a way, these maps also tell us the story of functioning of federalism in India.

The text explains that after studying this chapter you will be able to understand the following: ± what is Federalism; ± the federal provisions in the Indian Constitution; ± the issues involved in the relations between the centre and the States; and ± the special provisions for certain States having a distinct composition and historical features.

The text explains that 153 Chapter 7: Federalism WHAT IS FEDERALISM?

The text explains that uSSR was one of the world’s super powers, but after 1989 it simply broke up into several independent countries.

The text explains that one of the major reasons for its break up was the excessive centralisation and concentration of power, and the domination of Russia over other regions with independent languages and cultures of their own e.g.

The text explains that some other countries like Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, and Pakistan also had to face a division of the country.

The text explains that canada came very close to a break up between the English-speaking and the French-speaking regions of that country.

The text explains that can we attribute it to the federal structure of governance that we in India adopted through our Constitution?

The text explains that therefore, apart from adopting a federal constitution, the nature of that federal system and the practice of federalism must also be important factors.

The text explains that it had a weak central government and the economy of each unit was independent.

The text explains that these features and political competition among the units led to the formal dissolution of the federation in 1962.

The text explains that the Caribbean Community has even a common executive, and Heads of the governments of member countries are members of this executive.

The text explains that 154 Indian Constitution at Work India is a land of continental proportions and immense diversities.

The text explains that we have also participated in a common history, especially, when we fought for independence.

The text explains that rather, federalism as a principle of government has evolved differently in different situations.

The text explains that each government is autonomous in its own sphere.

The text explains that in some federal countries, there is even a system of dual citizenship.

The text explains that each level of the polity has distinct powers and responsibilities and has a separate system of government. ± The details of this dual system of government are generally spelt out in a written constitution, which is considered to be supreme and which is also the source of the power of both sets of government.

The text explains that certain subjects, which concern the nation as a whole, for example, defence or currency, are the responsibility of the union or central government.

The text explains that 155 Chapter 7: Federalism matters are the responsibility of the regional or State government. ± To prevent conflicts between the centre and the State, there is an independent judiciary to settle disputes.

The text explains that the judiciary has the powers to resolve disputes between the central government and the States on legal matters about the division of power.

The text explains that real politics, culture, ideology and history determine the actual working of a federation.

The text explains that political parties also determine the way a constitution would work.

The text explains that yes, I remember what we read in the first chapter: a constitution decides who should have how much power.

The text explains that 156 Indian Constitution at Work Federalism in Nigeria If the regions and various communities do not trust each other, even a federal arrangement can fail to produce unity.

The text explains that at the Ibadan Constitutional Conference of 1950 Nigerian leaders decided to form a federal constitution.

The text explains that in the 1960 constitution, both federal and regional governments jointly controlled the Nigerian police.

The text explains that in the military-supervised constitution of 1979, no state was allowed to have any civil police.

The text explains that though democracy was restored in Nigeria in 1999, religious differences along with conflicts over who will control revenues from the oil resources continue to present problems before the Nigerian federation.

The text explains that local ethnic communities resist centralised control of the oil resources.

The text explains that check your progress ± Who decides the powers of the central government in a federation? ± How are conflicts between the central government and the States resolved in a federation?

The text explains that 157 Chapter 7: Federalism FEDERALISM IN THE INDIAN CONSTITUTION Even before Independence, most leaders of our national movement were aware that to govern a large country like ours, it would be necessary to divide the powers between provinces and the central government.

The text explains that there was also awareness that Indian society had regional diversity and linguistic diversity.

The text explains that this was only logical if we wanted a democratic government.

The text explains that the only question was what should be the extent of powers to be enjoyed by the regional governments.

The text explains that once the decision to partition India was taken, the Constituent Assembly decided to frame a government that would be based on the principles of unity and cooperation between the centre and the States and separate powers to the States.

The text explains that the most important feature of the federal system adopted by the Indian Constitution is the principle that relations between the States and the centre would be based on cooperation.

The text explains that thus, while recognising diversity, the Constitution emphasised unity.

The text explains that why are many States unhappy about the role of the Governor?

The text explains that president’s rule can be imposed in a State if the government is not being run according to the provisions of the Constitution.

The text explains that state whether any of the following conditions are a fit case for imposition of President’s rule in the State.

The text explains that give reasons. √ two members of the State legislative assembly belonging to the main opposition party have been killed by criminals and the opposition is demanding dismissal of the State government. √ Kidnapping of young children for ransom is on rise.

The text explains that the number of crimes against women are increasing. √ No political party has secured majority in the recent elections of the State Legislative Assembly.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 7: Federalism

prelims_traps: Do not treat FEDERALISM and INTRODUCTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Federalism; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Federalism through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use FEDERALISM and INTRODUCTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
